content.text: What it is

SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts, pronounced "salsa") is a security framework that defines four levels of build integrity guarantees. SLSA provenance is a machine-verifiable attestation that describes how a software artifact (container image, compiled binary, Python wheel) was built: which source code was used, on which build platform, with which inputs, and by which process. For GenAI pipelines, the provenance attestation additionally records the model weight checksum and prompt template git hash as materials, creating a verifiable chain of custody from code to deployed artifact.

A SLSA provenance attestation is a signed JSON document in the in-toto attestation format, attached to the artifact in an OCI registry or published to a transparency log (Rekor). It can be cryptographically verified at deploy time to confirm that the artifact was built by a trusted build system from the expected source.
Why it matters

Software supply chain attacks — where an attacker compromises a build pipeline to inject malicious code into an artifact — are a primary threat vector for modern software. The SolarWinds and 3CX attacks demonstrated that trusted build pipelines can be used to distribute malware at scale. SLSA provenance provides a way to verify that a deployed artifact was built by a specific, trusted builder from a specific, known source — making it much harder for a supply-chain attack to go undetected.

For GenAI systems, the threat model extends to model weights and prompt templates. A tampered model weight file or a modified system prompt that was injected after the artifact was signed would not match the hashes recorded in the provenance attestation, causing verification to fail at deploy time and preventing the compromised artifact from reaching production.
Benefits
Tamper detection.
Any modification to the artifact after it was signed — whether to the application code, model weights, or prompt templates — causes provenance verification to fail at deploy time.
Build provenance audit trail.

Every deployed artifact has a signed record of which source commit, builder, and inputs produced it. This enables forensic analysis after an incident without relying on build logs that could themselves be tampered.
Compliance evidence.
EO 14028, NIST SSDF, and emerging EU Cyber Resilience Act requirements reference SLSA as a recommended framework. SLSA L2+ attestations satisfy these requirements.
Model integrity verification.
Recording model weight hashes in provenance materials enables verification that the model in production is the approved, evaluated version and has not been substituted.
Trade-offs and limitations
SLSA L3+ requires hardened build infrastructure.
Level 3 requires the build to run in an isolated, ephemeral environment inaccessible to developers. This rules out self-hosted build agents where developers have shell access.
Model weight hashing is expensive.
Computing SHA256 of multi-gigabyte model weight files adds minutes to build time. Cache the hash or pre-compute it during model publishing.
Verification adds deploy latency.
Fetching and verifying provenance attestations at deploy time adds 5-15 seconds per artifact. For large-scale deployments with hundreds of pods, this compounds.
Not a substitute for build security.

SLSA provenance proves how an artifact was built; it does not prove the source code or model weights are free of vulnerabilities. SBOM scanning addresses vulnerabilities; SLSA addresses build integrity.
How it works

During a CI build, a provenance generator (running as a trusted component of the CI platform or as a GitHub Actions step using the SLSA GitHub Generator) produces a provenance document that records: the build configuration source (the workflow file URI and commit), the builder identity (GitHub Actions runner, or a custom builder), and the materials list (all inputs to the build: source git commit SHA, model weight SHA256, prompt template git hash, and any other significant inputs).

The provenance document is signed using Cosign with keyless signing (Sigstore), which records the signing event in the Rekor public transparency log without requiring key management. The signed provenance is attached to the container image in the OCI registry as an attestation using cosign attest.

At deploy time, the CD pipeline runs slsa-verifier verify-artifact against the container image. The verifier fetches the attestation, verifies the signature against the Rekor log, and checks that the builder identity, source URI, and artifact hash match the expected values. If any check fails, the deployment is blocked.
Architecture

attrs.alt: SLSA provenance for GenAI pipelines: four SLSA levels (L1 generated, L2 signed, L3 hardened, L4 two-party). Center shows the provenance attestation structure with builder ID, build type, and materials including git SHA, model SHA, and prompt hash. Signed with Cosign via Sigstore Rekor. Right panel shows verification at deploy time: checks source URI, builder, artifact hash, model SHA, and prompt hash in materials. Failed checks block deployment.

content.text: The provenance attestation is the central artifact. It records the full build lineage including GenAI-specific materials. Verification at deploy time is the enforcement point that prevents tampered or incorrectly built artifacts from reaching production.
When to use it
For production GenAI systems with compliance requirements.
SLSA L2 (hosted build, signed provenance) is achievable with GitHub Actions or Google Cloud Build and satisfies most regulatory frameworks.
When model weights are fetched from external sources.
Recording the model weight hash in provenance enables verification that the correct, evaluated model version is deployed.
For high-value or regulated workloads.
AI agents that write to databases, execute code, or handle sensitive data warrant SLSA L3+ where build isolation prevents developer modification of the build environment.
How to implement
Start at SLSA L2.
Use the SLSA GitHub Generator (slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator) to generate and sign provenance for container images built in GitHub Actions. This achieves L2 with minimal configuration.
Add GenAI materials.
Extend the provenance materials list to include the model weight SHA256 and prompt template git hash. Pass these as inputs to the SLSA generator action.
Enforce verification at deploy time.
Add a verification step in the CD pipeline before deploying to production. Use slsa-verifier verify-image and configure it to fail the deployment if verification fails.
Publish the expected builder and source URI.
Document in your deployment runbook which builder ID and source URI are trusted for each service. Verification must check against known-good values.
Store provenance alongside SBOM.
Both attestation types are attached to the OCI image. Together they provide full supply-chain visibility: SBOM for components, SLSA for build lineage.
Examples
GitHub Actions: SLSA provenance with GenAI materials

name: Build with SLSA Provenance

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    outputs:
      image: ${{ steps.build.outputs.image }}
      digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build and push image
        id: build
        run: |
          docker build -t ghcr.io/org/agent-service:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push ghcr.io/org/agent-service:${{ github.sha }}
          DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' ghcr.io/org/agent-service:${{ github.sha }} | cut -d@ -f2)
          echo "image=ghcr.io/org/agent-service" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "digest=$DIGEST" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      - name: Compute model weight SHA
        id: model
        run: echo "sha=$(sha256sum /path/to/model.bin | cut -d' ' -f1)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  provenance:
    needs: build
    permissions:
      actions: read
      id-token: write
      packages: write
    uses: slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0
    with:
      image: ${{ needs.build.outputs.image }}
      digest: ${{ needs.build.outputs.digest }}
      registry-username: ${{ github.actor }}
      # Additional GenAI materials
      compile-generator-and-slsa-inputs: |
        model_sha256=${{ needs.build.outputs.model_sha }}
        prompt_git_hash=${{ github.sha }}
    secrets:
      registry-password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
Verification at deploy time

# In the CD pipeline, before deploying to production:
- name: Verify SLSA provenance
  run: |
    slsa-verifier verify-image \
      --source-uri github.com/org/agent-service \
      --source-branch main \
      --builder-id https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0 \
      ghcr.io/org/agent-service@${{ inputs.digest }}
  # Fails the pipeline if:
  # - Builder is not the expected SLSA generator
  # - Source URI does not match
  # - Artifact hash does not match the signed provenance
Real-world example

The Google Cloud Build platform offers native SLSA L3 provenance generation for container builds. An AI platform team migrated their builds from self-hosted Jenkins to Cloud Build specifically to achieve L3 provenance for their GenAI services, which processed healthcare data and required evidence that artifacts were built in a controlled, audited environment inaccessible to individual developers.

They extended the provenance with custom materials for model weights (SHA256 of the model checkpoint fetched from their internal model registry) and prompt templates (the git commit hash of the prompts directory). At deploy time, a Kubernetes admission webhook called slsa-verifier before allowing pod creation in the production namespace. This prevented any deployment that did not originate from an approved Cloud Build pipeline from reaching production.
Common mistakes
Generating provenance without enforcing verification.
Provenance attestations that are generated but never verified at deploy time provide a compliance document but not supply-chain protection.
Not including GenAI-specific materials.
A provenance attestation that records only the source git hash but not the model weight checksum cannot detect a model substitution attack.
Self-hosted build agents for L3+ claims.
SLSA L3 requires the builder to be inaccessible to developers. A self-hosted runner that developers can SSH into cannot provide L3 guarantees.
Overly broad trusted source URIs.
Verifying that the source matches github.com/org/* rather than github.com/org/agent-service allows any repository in the organization to build the artifact.
Best practices
Target SLSA L2 as a minimum for all production GenAI services; pursue L3 for services processing sensitive data.
Include model weight SHA256 and prompt template git hash as provenance materials to detect GenAI-specific substitution attacks.
Enforce provenance verification at deploy time, not just attestation generation; unverified provenance provides no protection.
Use the SLSA GitHub Generator for GitHub Actions; it provides L3 guarantees using the hosted GitHub runner infrastructure.
Verify exact source URIs and builder IDs, not wildcard patterns, to prevent an attacker from satisfying verification from an unintended source.
Store SLSA provenance alongside SBOM attestations in the OCI registry for a complete supply-chain audit trail.
Performance and scalability

SLSA provenance generation adds approximately 30-60 seconds to a build (provenance generation plus signing). Verification at deploy time adds 5-15 seconds. For large image files, computing SHA256 of model weights dominates build time; pre-compute and cache the hash during model publishing rather than recomputing on every build. Use parallel attestation steps in CI to overlap SBOM generation and provenance generation.
Enforcing verification with Kubernetes admission webhooks

Integrating SLSA verification into a Kubernetes admission webhook prevents pods without valid provenance from ever starting in a production cluster. The webhook intercepts pod creation requests and calls slsa-verifier (or a policy engine such as Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) before the Kubernetes scheduler schedules the pod. This creates a hard enforcement boundary: even if a developer pushes a built image directly to the container registry without going through the CI pipeline, it will not run in production because it lacks a valid signed provenance attestation.
Kyverno policy: require SLSA provenance

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-slsa-provenance
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-provenance
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
          namespaces:
            - production
      verifyImages:
        - imageReferences:
            - "ghcr.io/org/*"
          attestations:
            - predicateType: https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1
              conditions:
                - all:
                  - key: "{{ builder.id }}"
                    operator: Equals
                    value: "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@v2.0.0"
                  - key: "{{ buildDefinition.externalParameters.source.ref }}"
                    operator: Equals
                    value: "refs/heads/main"
          # Kyverno verifies the Sigstore/Cosign signature on the attestation
          # before evaluating the conditions. Unsigned or tampered attestations
          # are rejected before condition checking.
          attestors:
            - count: 1
              entries:
                - keyless:
                    subject: "https://github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-github-generator/.github/workflows/generator_container_slsa3.yml@refs/tags/v2.0.0"
                    issuer: "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
                    rekor:
                      url: https://rekor.sigstore.dev

With this policy applied, any pod that references an image without a matching SLSA attestation is rejected at admission with a policy violation message that identifies which condition failed. The Kyverno policy also validates the Sigstore keyless signature on the attestation before checking conditions, ensuring the attestation itself has not been tampered with after the build.

For teams using OPA Gatekeeper, an equivalent ConstraintTemplate can be authored using rego policies that call out to an external webhook or use the cosign library to verify the attestation signature. The key principle is the same: block deployment at the admission control layer so no unverified artifact reaches a running pod.
Apply the policy in audit mode first.
Run in report-only mode for two weeks to identify all images that lack provenance before switching to Enforce mode.
Namespace-scope the policy.
Apply SLSA enforcement only to production namespaces; development and staging may allow unsigned images to reduce friction for exploratory work.
Cache Rekor lookups.
Admission webhooks that check Rekor for every pod creation add network latency. Cache verified image digest results for the lifetime of the deployment object.
Version-pin the builder ID.
Pin the builder ID to a specific tagged version of the SLSA generator (e.g., @v2.0.0) to prevent an attacker from publishing a malicious generator under the same URL with a later tag.
Document exemption procedures.
Establish a formal process for emergency bypasses (e.g., a critical hotfix deployed from an operator workstation). Log and review all exemptions within 24 hours.
Tooling and ecosystem

Verification
CLI tool for verifying SLSA attestations at deploy time; supports container images and blobs
Cosign (Sigstore)
Signing and attestation
Keyless signing using Rekor transparency log; attaches attestations to OCI images
Google Cloud Build
L3 build platform
Native SLSA L3 provenance for builds; isolated builder inaccessible to developers

No
Security and reliability

SLSA provenance is only as trustworthy as the builder that generates it. For L2, the builder must be a hosted service not modifiable by the code author. For L3, the builder must run in an isolated environment with no developer access. Keyless signing via Sigstore binds the signature to the CI job's OIDC identity token, which cannot be forged from outside the CI platform.

For reliability, provenance verification should fail gracefully: a network error fetching the attestation from the registry should produce a retryable failure, not a permanent block. Configure up to 3 retries with exponential backoff before treating a verification failure as definitive.
SLSA level progression strategy

Teams new to SLSA should not attempt to reach Level 3 or Level 4 in a single migration. The cost of implementing every requirement simultaneously — hermetic builds, reproducible outputs, two-party review for all changes — is prohibitive. A phased progression, starting with Level 1 to establish provenance documents and build a team understanding of the framework, provides immediate value while creating the foundation for higher levels in later quarters.
Level 1 (quarter 1): add SLSA provenance generation to the existing CI pipeline; no process changes required; immediate auditability gain
Level 2 (quarter 2): migrate builds to a hosted CI platform with non-falsifiable audit logs; retire any developer-run local builds that feed production
Level 3 (quarter 3-4): enforce hermetic builds via network egress blocking; validate reproducibility with a second independent build for comparison
Reassess the target level against the threat model at each milestone: not every system requires Level 3 or 4
Verifying provenance at deployment gates

Generating provenance documents is only useful if the deployment pipeline verifies them before allowing an artifact to proceed. Verification at the deployment gate confirms that the artifact was built by the expected pipeline, from the expected source commit, and that the provenance document itself was not tampered with. Without this check, provenance is documentation but not a control.

The slsa-verifier CLI from the SLSA project accepts an artifact path, a provenance file path, and the expected source URI. It verifies the Sigstore signature, confirms the build platform matches the policy, and checks the source repository URI. Integrate this check as a mandatory step in the deployment pipeline between the artifact download step and the deploy step. Any failure halts the deployment and pages the on-call engineer with the verification error.
Run slsa-verifier against every artifact at the staging deployment gate; repeat at the production promotion gate
Store the expected source URI and builder ID in a policy file versioned in the infrastructure repository, not in the application repository
Alert on any provenance document that is older than 24 hours relative to the deployment timestamp: stale provenance may indicate a re-packaged artifact
Key takeaways
SLSA provenance proves how an artifact was built (builder, source, inputs) and makes supply-chain attacks detectable at deploy time.
For GenAI systems, extend provenance materials with model weight SHA256 and prompt template git hash to detect model and prompt substitution attacks.
SLSA L2 (hosted build + signed provenance) is achievable with GitHub Actions or Cloud Build and satisfies most regulatory requirements.
Enforce provenance verification at deploy time; unverified provenance is compliance paperwork without security protection.
Self-hosted build agents cannot provide SLSA L3+ guarantees because developers can access and modify the build environment.
SLSA provenance and SBOM are complementary: SBOM documents components; SLSA documents how the artifact was assembled from those components.
